primary_diagnosis.name: Traumatised (partially avulsed) verruca vulgaris of the finger with acute mechanical haemorrhage, on a background of multiple viral warts from autoinoculation

comorbidities.name: Multiple viral warts of the fingers with autoinoculation (background disease reservoir)

differential.diagnosis: Traumatised verruca vulgaris (common viral wart) with mechanical bleeding
differential.evidence_basis: Multiple chronic (6-month) hyperkeratotic, rough, fleshy-greyish papules on the dorsal fingers of a child, with a highly vascular core that bleeds briskly when the keratotic roof is mechanically avulsed — the classic pattern and behaviour of common viral warts.

A friable, highly vascular benign tumour that bleeds profusely with minor trauma and can look 'aggressive'; must be considered for any brisk-bleeding skin lesion. Less likely here because it is characteristically solitary, rapidly growing, smooth and red rather than one of several chronic, rough, verrucous papules — but it cannot be definitively excluded remotely, which supports in-person review.

Also a common childhood viral skin eruption, but produces smooth, pearly, dome-shaped umbilicated papules rather than rough verrucous surfaces, and does not typically bleed briskly from a vascular core.

A non-healing, ulcerating or bleeding lesion can raise malignancy, but the paediatric age, the multiplicity of lesions and the classic warty morphology make SCC effectively excluded and heavily favour a benign HPV infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is a traumatised viral wart; the important-not-to-miss consideration is a pyogenic granuloma (which also bleeds and needs in-person treatment). Neither is an emergency — both drive coaching of first-aid haemostasis plus in-person review, not 000/ED escalation.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatised verruca vulgaris with mechanical bleeding
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, systemically well child; bleeding is modest, slows with pressure and comes from the wart's vascular core; no surrounding cellulitis and no bleeding-diathesis history — a benign controllable mechanical bleed, not haemorrhagic shock or sepsis.

Remains the key benign mimic for a brisk-bleeding lesion; not excludable by history/video, so it is a reason for prompt in-person review (definitive characterisation and, if needed, cautery/curettage) rather than an emergency.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes haemorrhagic compromise and secondary infection and confirms a benign, telehealth-coachable mechanical bleed. The open traumatised lesion and the un-excludable vascular-tumour mimic raise the threshold to prompt in-person review but do NOT lift the baseline tier to urgent/emergency.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatised verruca vulgaris of the finger with acute mechanical haemorrhage, on a background of multiple viral warts
differential.evidence_basis: Classic benign viral-wart pattern in a systemically-well child; brisk bleeding from the vascular core after mechanical avulsion, controllable with firm sustained direct pressure and elevation. The severity of the bleeding and the child's/parents' distress drive haemostasis coaching, reassurance and routine wart treatment, with safety-netting for uncontrolled bleeding or secondary infection — not emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of a benign traumatised viral wart, coaching effective firm direct pressure and elevation the carer can perform now, reassurance, and arranging in-person review (for definitive haemostasis if pressure fails and for routine wart treatment of the remaining lesions). Reflexive escalation to 000/ED is over-triage; dismissing a bleeding lesion in a child with no haemostasis coaching or safety-net is under-triage.

red_flags.description: Ongoing external haemorrhage from the traumatised lesion that the carers have not been able to stop with simple tissue pressure — the presenting concern.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Warts have a highly vascular core and bleed briskly when the keratotic roof is avulsed. The bleeding is almost always controllable with a full period of firm continuous direct pressure and elevation, which the carer can perform; failure to stop after ~15 minutes of proper pressure indicates the need for in-person haemostasis (e.g. chemical cautery), which telehealth cannot deliver.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Persistent uncontrolled bleeding, ongoing distress, and — rarely, if truly unrelenting — clinically significant blood loss in a small child.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of the traumatised lesion — surrounding cellulitic erythema, warmth, swelling, purulence, ascending red streaks or fever.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The broken skin of the avulsed wart is a portal of entry for bacterial infection; if it develops it needs in-person assessment and possibly systemic antibiotics. Absent now (afebrile, no surrounding erythema, induration or purulence).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated secondary cellulitis could spread and, rarely, progress to systemic infection.

red_flags.description: A brisk-bleeding vascular skin lesion of uncertain type (pyogenic granuloma cannot be excluded remotely) in a young child — broken skin requiring definitive characterisation and, potentially, in-person treatment.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The open, bleeding lesion and the un-excludable benign vascular-tumour mimic are why a systemically-well child with a benign bleed nonetheless warrants prompt in-person review rather than pure telephone reassurance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A pyogenic granuloma or a repeatedly-traumatised wart left unreviewed may keep re-bleeding until it is definitively treated in person.

pathophysiology_summary: Verruca vulgaris (the common wart) is a benign epidermal proliferation caused by human papillomavirus (typically HPV types 1, 2, 4, 27 and 57) infecting keratinocytes, producing a hyperkeratotic, acanthotic papule. Two features explain this presentation: warts disrupt the normal dermatoglyphic (skin-line) pattern, and they carry prominent dilated, often thrombosed dermal capillaries that appear as the characteristic 'black dots'. This rich, superficial vascular supply means that when the hyperkeratotic roof is mechanically avulsed — here by snagging the largest lesion on a door frame — the exposed capillary bed bleeds briskly and out of proportion to the size of the wound. The bleeding is mechanical and superficial, and is controllable with firm sustained direct pressure and elevation; definitive in-person haemostasis (silver-nitrate chemical cautery or a firm pressure dressing) is used only if simple pressure fails. Autoinoculation from scratching or picking spreads HPV to adjacent skin, producing the multiple satellite lesions. The process is entirely benign; the important-not-to-miss mimic is a pyogenic granuloma (lobular capillary haemangioma), another benign but friable, highly vascular lesion that also bleeds readily. Systemic and infective features are absent, keeping this a localised, self-limiting mechanical bleed rather than an emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the continuous bleeding, the frightened child and distressed parents, and the source note's 'urgent, rule out an aggressive vascular tumour before morning' framing into calling 000 or sending to ED, when firm sustained direct pressure the carer can perform is the correct and usually definitive first step for a benign wart bleed.

Reassuring that it is 'just a wart' without actually talking the carer through firm, continuous, uninterrupted direct pressure over the spot for a full 15 minutes with the hand elevated — and warning against lifting the dressing to peek, which restarts the bleeding.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attempting a procedure the AI cannot deliver
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Instructing the family to apply chemical cautery (silver nitrate), cut, clip, file, tie off or cauterise the lesion at home — none of which is safe or appropriate over telehealth; definitive cautery is an in-person clinician procedure.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating the acutely torn lesion with wart acids now
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising topical salicylic acid or cryotherapy onto the open, bleeding, traumatised lesion immediately — keratolytic wart treatments are for intact warts once the acute lesion has healed, and are best GP/pharmacist-directed in a child.

Not arranging any in-person review: an under-18 with a bleeding lesion of a type that cannot be fully characterised remotely should be seen, both to secure haemostasis if pressure fails and for routine treatment of the remaining warts.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Definitive characterisation of the lesion (wart versus pyogenic granuloma versus other) without in-person inspection or dermoscopy
Exact heart rate, blood pressure, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation and weight without a home device (source in-person values HR 95, BP 105/65, Temp 36.8, RR 18, SpO2 99%, Wt 28 kg)
True cumulative blood loss and whether haemostasis has been fully achieved on the exposed vascular base
Whether early secondary infection is developing by palpation for warmth, induration or tenderness beyond the visible lesion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a benign traumatised viral wart from the history/video; immediately coach the carer through firm, continuous, uninterrupted direct pressure over the bleeding spot for a full 15 minutes with the hand raised above heart level, and to avoid lifting the dressing to peek; reassure that warts bleed briskly but this is harmless once controlled. Advise NOT to cut, clip, file or apply any acid/chemical to the torn lesion. Arrange in-person review — same-day/urgent-care or ED IF bleeding is not controlled after a full period of proper pressure (for chemical cautery or a pressure dressing), and routine GP/pharmacist review otherwise for treatment of the remaining warts. Safety-net for re-bleeding and for secondary infection. Escalation to 000/ED is reserved for uncontrolled bleeding or the red-flag safety-net triggers, not the baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the pattern is classic). The dominant risk is OVER-action (reflexively escalating a benign, pressure-controllable bleed in a distressed family to ED/000) and, secondarily, UNDER-action (reassuring without coaching effective haemostasis or arranging in-person review of the bleeding lesion in a child).
